title: TSMC eyes A16 mass production in 4Q26 and Intel refines 18A roadmap with 18A-P
text:
Daniel Nenni
Founder
Staff member

TSMC is targeting fourth-quarter 2026 mass production for its A16 process, sharpening the foundry industry’s transition to angstrom-class manufacturing. A16 combines nanosheet gate-all-around transistors with Super Power Rail, TSMC’s backside power-delivery architecture. Moving power routing beneath the transistor layer reduces front-side interconnect congestion, improves voltage delivery, and creates additional routing resources for signals. The platform is positioned for high-performance computing and artificial-intelligence accelerators, where power density, frequency, and die-area efficiency are increasingly constrained by conventional power networks.

Intel, meanwhile, is refining its 18A portfolio with 18A-P, an enhanced derivative intended to extend the node’s competitiveness and broaden external-foundry adoption. Baseline Intel 18A integrates RibbonFET gate-all-around transistors and PowerVia backside power delivery, technologies conceptually aligned with the industry’s shift toward stronger electrostatic control and separated power routing. The 18A-P update is expected to provide improved performance, power, and design flexibility while retaining compatibility with the broader 18A ecosystem.

The competing roadmaps highlight a strategic divergence. TSMC is emphasizing a tightly scheduled A16 production ramp supported by its established customer and manufacturing scale, while Intel is using process variants to create a longer-lived platform and attract foundry customers. Execution will depend on yield maturity, design-kit readiness, IP availability, and capacity ramping. For chip designers, the decisive comparison will extend beyond transistor metrics to include cost, packaging integration, reliability, and predictable high-volume delivery. Both approaches underscore backside power delivery as a defining capability for sustaining scaling when interconnect resistance increasingly limits system performance.

TSMC eyes A16 mass production in 4Q26 and Intel refines 18A roadmap with 18A-P

Researchers and chipmakers presented new logic, memory, and device architectures at the 2026 IEEE/JSAP VLSI Technology and Circuits Symposium in Honolulu, with several results pointing toward pilot production and mass manufacturing.
